channel.title: Daily GovCon Briefing
channel.description: Daily synthesis of federal contracting news — acquisition policy, DoD and VA, federal AI policy — and what it means for contractors. Five to seven minutes. One voice. Weekdays.
channel.itunes:author: Mike Ross
channel.itunes:summary: Daily synthesis of federal contracting news and what it means for contractors.

item.title: GovCon today: June 30, 2026 — FedRAMP 20x Drops the Drawbridge
item.description: FedRAMP's finalized 2026 consolidated rules make 20x certification broadly available as of July 4th and mandatory by January 1st, 2027 — the single most consequential near-term authorization change for small AI-as-a-service firms selling to DoD and VA. But the civilian authorization wall coming down doesn't move DISA's IL-4 and IL-5 requirements, meaning DoD's internal ATO bottleneck survives intact. Today's episode closes the compliance-as-moat arc tracked all week and maps the trifecta — FedRAMP 20x, the OMB PQC mandate, and operational AI fielding — into a coherent near-term procurement pic
item.content:encoded:
FedRAMP 20x Drops the Drawbridge

FedRAMP's finalized 2026 consolidated rules make 20x certification broadly available as of July 4th and mandatory by January 1st, 2027 — the single most consequential near-term authorization change for small AI-as-a-service firms selling to DoD and VA. But the civilian authorization wall coming down doesn't move DISA's IL-4 and IL-5 requirements, meaning DoD's internal ATO bottleneck survives intact. Today's episode closes the compliance-as-moat arc tracked all week and maps the trifecta — FedRAMP 20x, the OMB PQC mandate, and operational AI fielding — into a coherent near-term procurement picture for FFP-preferring AI shops.

Featured story

  • FedRAMP 20x widely available to cloud services with release of 2026 consolidated rules — FedScoop

Also today

  • OMB tells agencies to begin executing PQC transition by 2027 — Federal News Network
  • The White House's post-quantum executive order is an important milestone. It's time to get to work — Federal News Network
  • Overland AI lands Pentagon contract to produce autonomous ground vehicles for Marine Corps — DefenseScoop
  • Agentic-AI tool aims to give US commanders new target options 'within seconds' — Washington Technology
  • Transcom seeks partners to study autonomous, cargo-moving drone boats for future ops — DefenseScoop
  • How the Defense Intelligence Agency is fast-tracking data and AI modernization — DefenseScoop
  • Israel's Smart Shooter sees c-UAS demand grow across US military: Exec — Breaking Defense
  • DHS moves on awards for $640M technical services pact — Washington Technology
  • Navy sees scores of applications for tech-focused Reserve unit, mirroring Army push — Breaking Defense
  • OPM's new suitability authority blurs the line between hiring vetting and employee discipline — Government Executive

item.title: GovCon today: June 29, 2026 — GSA's SEWP Absorption and the Access-Gate Economy
item.description: GSA's measured takeover of the $60B SEWP vehicle is less a continuity story than a consolidation with a gentle rollout schedule — and the transition period is the window for current awardees to set the norms. Paired with FedRAMP's innovation-filtering effect and government-gated AI model release regimes, today's episode argues that the competitive moat for small AI contractors is no longer technical capability: it is credential stack depth. The 23% small business prime share headline conceals a 2% drop in absolute dollars — the denominator is being compressed through strategic bundling that is
item.content:encoded:
GSA's SEWP Absorption and the Access-Gate Economy

GSA's measured takeover of the $60B SEWP vehicle is less a continuity story than a consolidation with a gentle rollout schedule — and the transition period is the window for current awardees to set the norms. Paired with FedRAMP's innovation-filtering effect and government-gated AI model release regimes, today's episode argues that the competitive moat for small AI contractors is no longer technical capability: it is credential stack depth. The 23% small business prime share headline conceals a 2% drop in absolute dollars — the denominator is being compressed through strategic bundling that is hard to protest and harder to reverse.

Featured story

  • GSA taking measured approach to SEWP takeover — Nextgov/FCW

Also today

  • GSA's centralization push is a return to its roots, not just a Trump priority — Government Executive [404bda1fd4]
  • Time for a Revolutionary MAS Overhaul — Federal News Network [7a6c12dec0]
  • Small business prime awards fell in fiscal 2025 — Washington Technology [1ab5fe1423]
  • DISA expands enterprise software push across DoD — Federal News Network [8db599df81]
  • FedRAMP Is filtering out innovation, not just risk — Washington Technology [90ca6173f1]
  • Anthropic Restores Mythos 5 Access for Select US Organizations After Government Approval — GovConWire [fed69a8b89]
  • OpenAI releases new GPT-5.6 model to select partners — Nextgov/FCW [9289356cb0]
  • How the Pentagon is shaping its next cyber strategy — Breaking Defense [98db9dfbc4]
  • Artificial intelligence is cutting months off nuclear licensing review times, official says — Government Executive [a133481e4f]
  • The Federal EHR: Real stories, real impact — VA News [0316ef868f]
  • Tech bills of the week: Labeling AI-generated content; AI standards for private sector companies; and more — Nextgov/FCW [ff7519a7ec]

item.title: GovCon today: June 26, 2026 — The FAR Rewrite's Cybersecurity Liability Trap
item.description: The FAR overhaul's failure to resolve controlled unclassified information responsibility is not a loose end — it is a structural pricing problem that transfers unquantified breach liability onto vendors operating under firm-fixed-price terms. For small AI shops handling sensitive DoD and VA data, this gap is embedded in nearly every delivery. Today's episode also covers the FY2027 AI budget surge, why the headline overstates small-firm opportunity, and what Army exercise failures mean for how to pitch AI work.
item.content:encoded:
The FAR Rewrite's Cybersecurity Liability Trap

The FAR overhaul's failure to resolve controlled unclassified information responsibility is not a loose end — it is a structural pricing problem that transfers unquantified breach liability onto vendors operating under firm-fixed-price terms. For small AI shops handling sensitive DoD and VA data, this gap is embedded in nearly every delivery. Today's episode also covers the FY2027 AI budget surge, why the headline overstates small-firm opportunity, and what Army exercise failures mean for how to pitch AI work.

Featured story

  • Federal acquisition rewrite leaves cybersecurity confusion unresolved — Government Executive

Also today

  • Agencies have four months to finalize quantum-ready migration plans — FedScoop
  • OMB issues instructions for agency migration to quantum-proof encryption — Nextgov/FCW
  • A $1.5 trillion defense budget: The IT programs contractors should be watching — Washington Technology
  • Trump's budget supplemental would secure billions for munitions, emerging defense tech — DefenseScoop
  • Agencies award $179B to small firms in 2025, down from 2024 — Federal News Network
  • KBR hires CEO, finance chief to lead government spinoff — Washington Technology
  • Army Air Assault brigade found AI tools ill-suited to tactical planning — Breaking Defense
  • CRS tried to use AI, but less than 3% of results met their standards, director says — FedScoop
  • AI has helped to slash nuclear licensing review times, NRC official says — Nextgov/FCW
  • As opposition mounts, House cancels vote on VA overhaul bill — Government Executive
  • The defense sector confronts the growing convergence of cyber and kinetic domains — DefenseScoop
  • 101st Airborne unit put 'steel' between soldiers and the breach, tested limits of AI in recent exercise — DefenseScoop

item.title: GovCon today: June 25, 2026 — SEWP VI: 2,100 Awardees, One Transfer in Progress
item.description: NASA's release of 2,100 SEWP VI awardees is the biggest vehicle event of the year for small AI contractors — but the bullish consensus misses a real transition risk: GSA's absorption of the vehicle introduces institutional-memory gaps that could compress task-order flow right when new awardees need momentum. Meanwhile, leadership instability across DoD and IC acquisition is widening award-to-performance timelines system-wide, and post-quantum cryptography is crossing from research horizon to active compliance deadline.
item.content:encoded:
SEWP VI: 2,100 Awardees, One Transfer in Progress

NASA's release of 2,100 SEWP VI awardees is the biggest vehicle event of the year for small AI contractors — but the bullish consensus misses a real transition risk: GSA's absorption of the vehicle introduces institutional-memory gaps that could compress task-order flow right when new awardees need momentum. Meanwhile, leadership instability across DoD and IC acquisition is widening award-to-performance timelines system-wide, and post-quantum cryptography is crossing from research horizon to active compliance deadline.

Featured story

  • NASA announces 2,100 awards under SEWP's sixth generation — FedScoop

Also today

  • GSA taking measured approach to SEWP takeover — Washington Technology [3131232250]
  • Just having a 'human in the loop' is not AI governance — FedScoop [974c4548aa]
  • Anthropic's Mythos model found vulnerabilities in classified US government systems, official says — Federal News Network [e20de41640]
  • Analysis: Promoting Innovation & Post-Quantum Cybersecurity with the Trump Administration's Quantum Leap — GovConWire [cdb6196769]
  • DOD quantum strategy 'a first step' in preparing for the future, CIO says — Nextgov/FCW [605a2dc11b]
  • Senator plans to propose quantum initiative reauthorization as part of NDAA — Nextgov/FCW [a4b155042f]
  • Trump's pick for defense acquisition deputy lacks traditional resume — Federal News Network [e9eb34cfdb]
  • Space Force acquisition nominee faces ethics scrutiny over defense industry ties — Government Executive [62154f7765]
  • ODNI deputy director pushed out amid Pulte cuts — Nextgov/FCW [f61d481ae9]

item.title: GovCon today: June 24, 2026 — GSA Writes the AI Compliance Rulebook
item.description: GSA has proposed the first AI-specific acquisition clause in federal contracting history, imposing a four-role taxonomy — developer, operator, integrator, service provider — with distinct flow-down requirements on every GSA vehicle prime. The rule lands on top of an open FAR comment window, compressing the timeline in which small AI contractors must engage on both foundational procurement reform and new AI-specific compliance requirements simultaneously. The comment period closes in mid-August; the language finalized there will govern AI task orders on the Federal Supply Schedule, GWACs, and O
item.content:encoded:
GSA Writes the AI Compliance Rulebook

GSA has proposed the first AI-specific acquisition clause in federal contracting history, imposing a four-role taxonomy — developer, operator, integrator, service provider — with distinct flow-down requirements on every GSA vehicle prime. The rule lands on top of an open FAR comment window, compressing the timeline in which small AI contractors must engage on both foundational procurement reform and new AI-specific compliance requirements simultaneously. The comment period closes in mid-August; the language finalized there will govern AI task orders on the Federal Supply Schedule, GWACs, and OASIS+ for years.

Featured story

  • GSA unveils new AI-specific acquisition rule — Washington Technology

Also today

  • 30 days to shape the biggest FAR overhaul in 40 years — Washington Technology
  • Federal acquisition overhaul moves from plan to proposed rules — Government Executive
  • House passes bill to force SBA's hand on AI reporting — FedScoop
  • Army Exploring Rapid Acquisition Authorities to Move Faster With AI — GovConWire
  • Marine Corps mandates use of new digital app for SITREPs — DefenseScoop
  • Army will 'open up' ranges for defense vendors to speed up testing, with some sites mimicking Ukrainian frontlines — DefenseScoop
  • Parts of NSA lose Mythos 5 access amid Anthropic supply chain dispute — Nextgov/FCW
  • More SEWP for contractors as NASA expands total awards — Federal News Network
  • Outdated, conflicting guidance causes cloud procurement problems, watchdog says — FedScoop
  • NDAA provisions would reshape Pentagon's use of ownership stakes in private companies — Washington Technology
  • Watchdog review sheds new light on DOGE's downsizing impacts at DOD — DefenseScoop
  • Securing Global Dominance: DoW Unleashes Quantum Defense Strategy to Harden Networks and Empower the Joint Force — DoD News Releases

item.title: GovCon today: June 23, 2026 — GAO on the Chopping Block
item.description: The FAR overhaul's 1,000-plus pages hit the Federal Register this week, and the most consequential provision isn't the plain-language rewrite — it's the Part 33 proposal to strip GAO of bid protest jurisdiction and move it inside procuring agencies. For small FFP-preferring AI contractors, GAO is the last independent check on large-incumbent wins; losing it while prime consolidation accelerates is a structural double hit. The comment window is open now, and a sparse record is a gift to the provision's proponents.
item.content:encoded:
GAO on the Chopping Block

The FAR overhaul's 1,000-plus pages hit the Federal Register this week, and the most consequential provision isn't the plain-language rewrite — it's the Part 33 proposal to strip GAO of bid protest jurisdiction and move it inside procuring agencies. For small FFP-preferring AI contractors, GAO is the last independent check on large-incumbent wins; losing it while prime consolidation accelerates is a structural double hit. The comment window is open now, and a sparse record is a gift to the provision's proponents.

Featured story

  • Over 1,000 pages on FAR overhaul heads to formal rulemaking process — FedScoop

Also today

  • Revolutionary FAR Overhaul moves to formal rulemaking with first batch of proposed rules — Nextgov/FCW
  • Executive order jumpstarts Pentagon's quantum sensor projects — Breaking Defense
  • Trump signs 2 orders to prepare the US for a quantum future — Nextgov/FCW
  • Booz Allen to acquire Ultra defense tech unit for $720M — Washington Technology
  • Army taps Anduril as lead for NGC2 common data layer baseline as service sets 'groundwork for rapid scaling' — DefenseScoop
  • Army picks Anduril to lead Next Gen C2 common data layer baseline — Breaking Defense
  • NASA Names 364 Initial Awardees for SEWP VI IT Contract Vehicle — GovConWire
  • Battelle challenges KBR's $8B Antarctica contract win — Washington Technology
  • Senate defense bill seeks to attract cyber talent, limit civilian layoffs — Federal News Network
  • Army looking toward autonomous robots to recover its downed vehicles from combat zones — DefenseScoop
  • Why Trump invoked the Defense Production Act now — Breaking Defense
  • Pentagon's growing role as an investor draws new scrutiny on Capitol Hill — Government Executive

item.title: GovCon today: June 22, 2026 — FAR Rulemaking Drops the Hammer
item.description: Seventeen FAR parts just entered formal notice-and-comment rulemaking — the biggest structural acquisition event since 1984. Today's episode argues that the consensus narrative (FAR reform burdens small firms, benefits large primes) is wrong for AI-native shops: if Part 12 commercial-item treatment comes out strengthened, small FFP-oriented AI firms may face lower compliance burden on commercial buys, not higher. The firms most exposed are mid-tier IT services primes living on FAR complexity, and VARs being disintermediated are the canary.
item.content:encoded:
FAR Rulemaking Drops the Hammer

Seventeen FAR parts just entered formal notice-and-comment rulemaking — the biggest structural acquisition event since 1984. Today's episode argues that the consensus narrative (FAR reform burdens small firms, benefits large primes) is wrong for AI-native shops: if Part 12 commercial-item treatment comes out strengthened, small FFP-oriented AI firms may face lower compliance burden on commercial buys, not higher. The firms most exposed are mid-tier IT services primes living on FAR complexity, and VARs being disintermediated are the canary.

Featured story

  • First 17 parts of the FAR move into formal rulemaking process — Federal News Network

Also today

  • What's next for value-added resellers? — Federal News Network [6723e8508e]
  • Senate lawmakers bring back acquisition reforms dropped from final 2026 NDAA — Federal News Network [a3496ad8e5]
  • Pentagon's growing role as an investor draws new scrutiny on Capitol Hill — Government Executive [69aaf8a0d2]
  • Why Trump invoked the Defense Production Act now — Breaking Defense [741c85cbd2]
  • Office of Naval Research rolls out new science and technology strategy — Federal News Network [d0dcb0867a]
  • Cloud Exchange 2026: GSA's Jessie Posilkin on how TMF investments help accelerate modernization — Federal News Network [bc9c1cbc9c]
  • Automation vs autonomy: What will shape the future of battlefield drones? — Breaking Defense [5821cd85e2]

item.title: GovCon today: June 19, 2026 — VA Names the Stakes: AI Capability or Lose the Contract
item.description: The VA's principal deputy assistant secretary for IT told contractors this week that incumbency is no longer a shield — the agency will review contracts at any point based on evolving requirements, not just performance failures. Today's episode argues that this is the civilian-agency confirmation of a week-long pattern: governance debt from rushed AI deployment commitments is now arriving as concrete procurement consequences across DoD, VA, and DHS simultaneously. For small FFP AI shops, the directional signal is real — but near-term cash-flow risk from VA institutional instability means the o
item.content:encoded:
VA Names the Stakes: AI Capability or Lose the Contract

The VA's principal deputy assistant secretary for IT told contractors this week that incumbency is no longer a shield — the agency will review contracts at any point based on evolving requirements, not just performance failures. Today's episode argues that this is the civilian-agency confirmation of a week-long pattern: governance debt from rushed AI deployment commitments is now arriving as concrete procurement consequences across DoD, VA, and DHS simultaneously. For small FFP AI shops, the directional signal is real — but near-term cash-flow risk from VA institutional instability means the opportunity is quarters away, not imminent.

Featured story

  • VA IT official to contractors: Bring your AI game or get axed — FedScoop

Also today

  • Transparency coming to OTAs, value of VARs — Federal News Network [6715ddcf65]
  • Defense contractor settles cybersecurity False Claims Act allegations — DefenseScoop [cc8284655f]
  • The Genesis Mission has a security problem — Washington Technology [bfd80bcbf2]
  • Golden Dome Update: Congressional Scrutiny, Industry Opportunities & New Acquisition Approaches — GovConWire [b2987ab667]
  • DOD excels at prototyping. Getting to production is another story. — Washington Technology [32d76bdc7e]
  • DHS contractors are still struggling to turn funding into actual work and payment — Federal News Network [07729d41f7]
  • Planned NDAA amendment would codify CISA's role in cyber vulnerability program — Nextgov/FCW [d285cef9a4]
  • Stop measuring AI by speed. Start measuring it by scores. — Federal News Network [11fcaf2a24]
  • The Pentagon is spending billions on major weapons systems, but there are growing questions — Federal News Network [6a1d9e5c84]
  • Navigating risk in ERP modernization to deliver long-term value — Federal News Network [80f1d40c0e]

item.title: GovCon today: June 18, 2026 — Drones Go to Production; Software Is the Open Door
item.description: The Air Force's ahead-of-schedule CCA production awards to Anduril and General Atomics confirm that DoD's autonomous systems market has crossed from prototype to production — and the real contracting opportunity now lies in the parallel autonomy software competition, sustainment analytics, and mission-systems integration work flowing behind the airframes. Meanwhile, Army Orchestra, Navy S&T at Speed, and GSA's updated CO guidance are collectively operationalizing the administration's FFP and commercial-item directive, opening structural on-ramps for agile small firms — but only if they are pos
item.content:encoded:
Drones Go to Production; Software Is the Open Door

The Air Force's ahead-of-schedule CCA production awards to Anduril and General Atomics confirm that DoD's autonomous systems market has crossed from prototype to production — and the real contracting opportunity now lies in the parallel autonomy software competition, sustainment analytics, and mission-systems integration work flowing behind the airframes. Meanwhile, Army Orchestra, Navy S&T at Speed, and GSA's updated CO guidance are collectively operationalizing the administration's FFP and commercial-item directive, opening structural on-ramps for agile small firms — but only if they are positioned before the vehicle landscape solidifies.

Featured story

  • GA-ASI, Anduril Awarded Air Force CCA Production Contracts — GovConWire

Also today

  • Air Force picks Anduril, General Atomics to build first operational CCA drones — DefenseScoop
  • How one US Army brigade is learning to sacrifice robots in lieu of humans — Breaking Defense
  • Marine Corps activates first unmanned maintenance squadron to repair its own MQ-9A Reaper drones — DefenseScoop
  • Army unveils first sketch of commercial tech contract for test, evaluation — Washington Technology
  • Navy preps science-and-tech strategy built for speed and focus — Washington Technology
  • A new paradigm as GSA looks to loosen the reins on contracting officers — within reason — Federal News Network
  • HHS issues call for AI to support its 'power users' — Nextgov/FCW
  • EPA piloting AI on 'everything,' but experts still needed for highest levels — FedScoop
  • CISA now has full Mythos Preview access, people familiar say — Nextgov/FCW
  • OpenAI's ChatGPT to debut on GenAI.mil in 'early July' — Washington Technology
  • Senate NDAA proposes CMMC grant program — Federal News Network
  • Senate Armed Services Committee releases NDAA details — Federal News Network
  • Unanet GAUGE Report Finds GovCon Confidence Slipping as Top Contractors Double Down on AI, Operational Discipline — GovConWire
  • DoD's $9.7B award for Microsoft products derailed by protest — Federal News Network
  • VA's Federal EHR rollout success continues in Southern Ohio — VA News
  • Proposed Rule: General Services Acquisition Regulation; Acquisition of Information and Communication Technology — Federal Register

item.title: GovCon today: June 17, 2026 — Agentic AI Deploys; Accountability Doesn't
item.description: Federal agencies are fielding agentic AI systems that initiate real-world actions inside government workflows — and almost none of them can consistently reconstruct how an output was formed, what data it touched, or what permissions enabled it. For small FFP-preferring AI shops, the governance gap isn't a policy problem to wait out: it's an unpriced liability surface that lands on the contractor when the audit trail disappears. Today's episode maps that risk and explains the PWS language that can scope it out.
item.content:encoded:
Agentic AI Deploys; Accountability Doesn't

Federal agencies are fielding agentic AI systems that initiate real-world actions inside government workflows — and almost none of them can consistently reconstruct how an output was formed, what data it touched, or what permissions enabled it. For small FFP-preferring AI shops, the governance gap isn't a policy problem to wait out: it's an unpriced liability surface that lands on the contractor when the audit trail disappears. Today's episode maps that risk and explains the PWS language that can scope it out.

Featured story

  • Agentic AI is coming to government faster than its guardrails — FedScoop

Also today

  • OpenAI's ChatGPT to debut on GenAI.mil in 'early July' — Nextgov/FCW
  • AI is taking background checks from 'months to hours,' clearance agency says — Government Executive
  • 3 priorities for federal CISOs in the agentic era — Nextgov/FCW
  • DIU leans into risk to field commercial tech faster — Federal News Network
  • Data from 'half a million hours of Ukraine conflict drone footage' now available to train AI — DefenseScoop
  • GOP's VA overhaul bill narrows some employees' rights, spurs privatization, union says — Government Executive
  • DISA Seeks Sources for $850M Army Endpoint Security Management Requirement — GovConWire
  • Trump administration builds on Tech Force model to recruit IT workers for short-term jobs — Federal News Network
  • Proposed Rule: General Services Acquisition Regulation; Acquisition of Information and Communication Technology; Notice of Listening Sessions and Request for Comments — Federal Register
  • DARPA exploring tech for tactically responsive space operations — DefenseScoop
  • Federal contracting is heading into a different kind of environment — Federal News Network

item.title: GovCon today: June 16, 2026 — The Government Is Blocking Its Own AI Strategy
item.description: Commerce export controls on Anthropic's Fable 5 model are now the sharpest concrete collision between U.S. AI competitiveness policy and DoD's declared AI-first acquisition strategy — and they extend the governance-lag arc this show has tracked from the Mythos freeze through today. The administration is issuing aggressive mandates on AI access, cyber timelines, and small-business eligibility without the implementation infrastructure to execute them, and the firms that pre-position now — on the right vehicles, with model-agnostic architectures — will capture the resulting emergency spend.
item.content:encoded:
The Government Is Blocking Its Own AI Strategy

Commerce export controls on Anthropic's Fable 5 model are now the sharpest concrete collision between U.S. AI competitiveness policy and DoD's declared AI-first acquisition strategy — and they extend the governance-lag arc this show has tracked from the Mythos freeze through today. The administration is issuing aggressive mandates on AI access, cyber timelines, and small-business eligibility without the implementation infrastructure to execute them, and the firms that pre-position now — on the right vehicles, with model-agnostic architectures — will capture the resulting emergency spend.

Featured story

  • Industry and academia call on administration to free Anthropic's AI model — Nextgov/FCW

Also today

  • How the Commerce crackdown on Anthropic could impact the Pentagon: Experts — Breaking Defense
  • 5 Army AI Initiatives Transforming Soldier Safety, Training, Cyber Defense & Acquisition — GovConWire
  • 5 initiatives driving the Marines to be an AI-first force — Federal News Network
  • Trump memo sets 'aggressive' timelines to secure sensitive systems — Federal News Network
  • NSPM-12: The NSS cyber memo agencies cannot ignore — Nextgov/FCW
  • Warner presses CISA on whether staff cuts weakened regional cyber support — Nextgov/FCW
  • SBA proposes opening 8(a) program to white-owned businesses — Washington Technology
  • Alaska speaks with one voice about the 8(a) program — Federal News Network
  • Policy hurdles, disconnect with Pentagon office lead to 'mixed success' for major SOCOM programs, watchdog says — DefenseScoop
  • Agencies are doubling down on AI upskilling, but they may be solving the wrong problem — Federal News Network
  • Europe rearms: Battle management, counter-UAS, and real AI at the edge — Breaking Defense

item.title: GovCon today: June 15, 2026 — The FFP Mandate's Hidden Trap
item.description: Trump's fixed-price and AI executive orders are nominally favorable for FFP-capable small AI contractors — but the Professional Services Council is publicly flagging implementation ambiguity that could let agencies route developmental AI work back to cost-type vehicles through a complexity carve-out. Today's episode traces that risk through the PSC's WT 360 analysis, connects it to the VA's ungoverned AI adoption failure, and argues that the Washington Technology Top 100 'resilience' narrative is masking a defense-civilian bifurcation that small AI shops straddling DoD and VA need to reckon wi
item.content:encoded:
The FFP Mandate's Hidden Trap

Trump's fixed-price and AI executive orders are nominally favorable for FFP-capable small AI contractors — but the Professional Services Council is publicly flagging implementation ambiguity that could let agencies route developmental AI work back to cost-type vehicles through a complexity carve-out. Today's episode traces that risk through the PSC's WT 360 analysis, connects it to the VA's ungoverned AI adoption failure, and argues that the Washington Technology Top 100 'resilience' narrative is masking a defense-civilian bifurcation that small AI shops straddling DoD and VA need to reckon with now.

Featured story

  • WT 360: Key points (and questions too) from Trump's fixed-price contracting and AI orders — Washington Technology

Also today

  • VA clinical staff rushed to use generative AI without oversight, watchdog finds — FedScoop [61dedbc5cb]
  • VA's AI chatbots not designated high-impact, despite clinical use, watchdog says — Nextgov/FCW [f0f065412a]
  • AWS Lands Potential $2.6B DHS Cumulus Cloud Acquisition Contract — GovConWire [bcb1c60b7b]
  • Navy awards 59 seats on $249M logistics IT contract — Washington Technology [8aad6508ca]
  • DLA Seeks Offers for Potential $903M ERP Cloud Hosting Support IDIQ — GovConWire [e25e5e40bd]
  • Pentagon may 'sacrifice' traditional weapons to buy more drones if reconciliation fails: CTO — Breaking Defense [7c17d4ca45]
  • Senator questions Pentagon's plan to revise autonomous weapons policy — DefenseScoop [a5b5cca2ca]
  • National Security Presidential Memorandum/NSPM-12 — White House Presidential Actions [d2887b6254]
  • The 2026 Top 100 shows a market that bent, but did not break — Washington Technology [ef50f02b1a]
  • How DHA plans to end Leidos' run as the military's health record integrator — Washington Technology [43bf2f78d0]
  • White House cyber office hire triggers leadership changes inside infrastructure security agency — Government Executive [39d80c95ec]
  • CISA sees leadership shakeup after infrastructure security chief moves to ONCD — Nextgov/FCW [7cb6c3a377]
  • CISA revives push toward long-awaited cyber incident reporting rules — Federal News Network [1d5ae24763]
  • SBA kicks off new audit of economically disadvantaged contractors — Federal News Network [5445dc46b4]
  • What GovCons Need to Know About the Army's New 'Amazon-Like' UAS Marketplace — GovConWire [ef054e4f9b]
  • Anthropic suspends top AI models after U.S. export control order — Nextgov/FCW [0f015f890b]
  • SASC proposes reorganization of Pentagon's IT, cyber leadership — DefenseScoop [14d03c3c3f]

item.title: GovCon today: June 12, 2026 — NITAAC's Final Gate Closes
item.description: NITAAC has confirmed the sunset of its entire GWAC portfolio — CIO-SP3, CIO-SP3 Small Business, and CIO-CS — with October 29, 2026 as the hard stop for new order awards. For small AI contractors, this is a structural, irreversible narrowing of the task order market, not a political headwind that can be unwound. The firms that clear the next decade of federal AI work are the ones already on Polaris or holding Alliant 2 teaming arrangements — vehicle position is now the primary competitive differentiator, ahead of technical capability.
item.content:encoded:
NITAAC's Final Gate Closes

NITAAC has confirmed the sunset of its entire GWAC portfolio — CIO-SP3, CIO-SP3 Small Business, and CIO-CS — with October 29, 2026 as the hard stop for new order awards. For small AI contractors, this is a structural, irreversible narrowing of the task order market, not a political headwind that can be unwound. The firms that clear the next decade of federal AI work are the ones already on Polaris or holding Alliant 2 teaming arrangements — vehicle position is now the primary competitive differentiator, ahead of technical capability.

Featured story

  • NIH contracting arm announces sunset of all governmentwide vehicles — FedScoop

Also today

  • White House discussions are weighing giving CISA Mythos access — Washington Technology
  • AI can save veterans' time, but TMF head says reauthorization needed for more investments — FedScoop
  • Senate pushes DOD to create new combatant command for unmanned systems — DefenseScoop
  • SASC's $1.14T defense policy bill creates combatant command for drones — Breaking Defense
  • White House polices hitting small business contractors hard, Senator says — Federal News Network
  • Air Force Releases Draft RFP for TETRAS III R&D Support IDIQ — GovConWire
  • Navy Taps 59 Companies for Potential $250M Logistics IT Modernization Contract — GovConWire
  • Army commissions 3 more tech executives into Detachment 201 — DefenseScoop

item.title: GovCon today: June 11, 2026 — The Mythos Governance Freeze
item.description: The White House hasn't told agencies how to access or deploy Anthropic's Mythos model — and that silence is now colliding with CISA's hard-deadline vulnerability-patching mandates, creating a mandate-without-tool condition that is a direct pricing and scheduling risk for any AI shop bidding on federal cyber work. Meanwhile, GWAC consolidation is accelerating and SBA on-ramps are disappearing: the channel problem is real, but total federal IT spend isn't shrinking — it's being redistributed toward large platforms and surviving vehicles.
item.content:encoded:
The Mythos Governance Freeze

The White House hasn't told agencies how to access or deploy Anthropic's Mythos model — and that silence is now colliding with CISA's hard-deadline vulnerability-patching mandates, creating a mandate-without-tool condition that is a direct pricing and scheduling risk for any AI shop bidding on federal cyber work. Meanwhile, GWAC consolidation is accelerating and SBA on-ramps are disappearing: the channel problem is real, but total federal IT spend isn't shrinking — it's being redistributed toward large platforms and surviving vehicles.

Featured story

  • Lack of White House guidance has complicated agency Mythos adoption, people familiar say — Washington Technology

Also today

  • CISA directive revamps how agencies prioritize vulnerable systems — Nextgov/FCW `[1a605b6c2b]`
  • CISA directive orders agencies to prioritize vulnerability patching in a new way — FedScoop `[922d754cf0]`
  • AI directive focuses patching efforts on 'highest risk' vulnerabilities — Federal News Network `[52ed252618]`
